behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix
behaviors.justification: Real filesystem file discovery and glob behavior is platform-dependent; mocked file lists hide ordering bugs in actual discovery logic. Needs real temp directory with actual .sql files to catch platform-specific filesystem behavior and sorting logic failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with .sql files (001-first.sql, 010-tenth.sql, 002-second.sql). Mock database inserts. Run migration runner and verify migrations are processed in numeric order (001, 002, 010) by checking the order of insert calls or side effects.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration-level with real files + mocked DB is appropriate. No real failure slips through because filesystem glob behavior is observable with real files.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run
behaviors.justification: Idempotency requires verifying that the actual migrations table query returns correct results and prevents re-execution. Mocking the query would hide bugs in the actual SQL logic, column names, or transaction isolation. Must use a real test database to catch query semantics and actual row state.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real test database. Run migrations once, verify _migrations table contains one record per migration. Run migrations a second time and verify: (1) _migrations table still has the same records, (2) no migration logic executes on the second run (via insertion count or mocked side effects).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is correct. Real database catches both query logic bugs and actual transaction semantics. No real failure slips through.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted
behaviors.justification: Transactional rollback is database-specific and can't be verified with mocks. Postgres transaction semantics, error handling, and actual data rollback are observable only with a real database process. Mocks always pass rollback checks; real databases may fail silently. SQLite and Postgres rollback behavior differs; System level ensures Postgres-specific transaction semantics are tested.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test database with one valid migration file and one migration file with a SQL syntax error. Run migration runner. Verify: (1) first migration's changes are present in the database, (2) second migration's changes are completely absent (rollback succeeded), (3) _migrations table records only the successful migration, not the failed one, (4) runner stops and reports the error without attempting further migrations.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System level is necessary. Integration with SQLite might hide Postgres-specific rollback or error handling bugs. Real database process semantics are essential.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances
behaviors.justification: Race conditions between concurrent processes can only be observed with real OS-level process spawning and real database locking. Mocking concurrency or serializing execution in a single test process can never reveal lock bugs or race windows. Real timing windows where both processes simultaneously detect 'migration not applied' only appear with true subprocess concurrency and real database serialization.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn two Node.js processes via child_process.spawn(), both pointing to the same test database. Coordinate them with a synchronization point (e.g., both wait for a signal file) to maximize race window overlap. Run migration discovery simultaneously. Verify _migrations table has exactly one record per migration file (no duplicates). Verify neither process inserted a duplicate metadata record, and both processes complete without error.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System level is mandatory. No simulation or mock can replace real process concurrency and real database locks. Integration-level test would serialize execution and hide race bugs.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied
behaviors.justification: Requires verifying both the order of operations (CREATE TABLE before first INSERT) and the actual table schema. Mocking the CREATE TABLE call would hide schema bugs (missing columns, wrong types, wrong constraints). Real test database verifies the table exists with the correct structure and that no migration code runs before table creation.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a fresh test database that has no _migrations table. Run migration runner. Verify: (1) _migrations table exists after the runner completes, (2) table has the correct schema (columns for migration name, timestamp, checksum/hash if applicable), (3) migrations are recorded in the table with correct values, (4) no migration-related operations happen before the table is created.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration level is appropriate. Real test database with schema verification catches both operation-order and schema bugs. No real failure slips through because table structure is directly observable.
